plainspeak.doctor: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers — a transformer-based language model pretrained on masked language modeling and next-sentence prediction tasks, producing deeply bidirectional contextualized word representations
plainspeak.high-school: A foundational language AI model that reads text in both directions simultaneously to understand word meaning from full context
plainspeak.kindergarten: A foundational language AI that reads words looking both left and right to understand what they mean in context

plainspeak.doctor: Vector policy optimization — an RL training method that optimizes for diversity across a vector reward space rather than a single scalar reward, improving test-time search performance
plainspeak.high-school: Training AI to produce many different kinds of good answers instead of optimizing for just one correct answer
plainspeak.kindergarten: Teaching AI to come up with lots of different good ideas instead of just one

plainspeak.doctor: Accumulated message effects on LLM judgments — the phenomenon where prior conversation history polarity systematically biases subsequent LLM-as-judge evaluations
plainspeak.high-school: The discovered effect where an AI's judgment in a conversation gets biased by what was said earlier in the same conversation
plainspeak.kindergarten: When what an AI has read before in a conversation changes how it judges the next thing you show it

plainspeak.doctor: Byte pair encoding — a subword tokenization algorithm that iteratively merges the most frequent byte or character pairs in a corpus to build a fixed-size vocabulary
plainspeak.high-school: Splitting words into smaller pieces by repeatedly merging the most common pair of characters or subwords
plainspeak.kindergarten: Breaking words into chunks by merging the most frequent letter pairs, one step at a time

plainspeak.doctor: Bits per byte — a compression efficiency metric measuring the average number of bits needed to encode one byte of text under a model's probability distribution
plainspeak.high-school: A metric for how efficiently a model compresses text — lower means the model predicts the text better
plainspeak.kindergarten: A score showing how well an AI can compress or predict text — lower scores mean it understands the text better

plainspeak.doctor: Human activity recognition — the task of identifying physical activities (walking, running, sitting) from sensor data such as accelerometers, gyroscopes, or video
plainspeak.high-school: Using sensors or cameras to identify what physical activity a person is doing — like walking, running, or sitting
plainspeak.kindergarten: A computer that can tell if you're walking, running, sitting, or dancing from sensor data

plainspeak.doctor: Natural language processing — a subfield of AI/CS that enables computers to understand, interpret, generate, and manipulate human language in text and speech
plainspeak.high-school: The field of AI focused on making computers understand, process, and communicate in human language
plainspeak.kindergarten: The field of teaching computers to understand, read, write, and talk in human language

plainspeak.doctor: Political consistency training — an RL training method that reduces covert political bias in LLMs by optimizing for symmetric sentiment and helpfulness across matched political prompts
plainspeak.high-school: A training method that teaches AI to respond consistently no matter how a question is politically framed
plainspeak.kindergarten: A training method that teaches AI to be fair and consistent instead of favoring one political side over the other

plainspeak.doctor: Retrieval-augmented generation — a technique that enhances LLM outputs by retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base and conditioning generation on them, improving factual accuracy and grounding
plainspeak.high-school: Giving an AI access to a searchable knowledge base so it can look up facts instead of relying only on its training data
plainspeak.kindergarten: Letting the AI look things up in a library before answering, instead of relying only on what it memorized

plainspeak.doctor: Speech emotion recognition — computational analysis of acoustic features (prosody, pitch, energy) to classify emotional states from spoken audio
plainspeak.high-school: Using computer analysis of tone of voice, pitch, and sound patterns to detect emotions in speech
plainspeak.kindergarten: A computer that can tell if you sound happy, sad, or angry from your voice

plainspeak.doctor: Inertial measurement unit — an electronic device combining accelerometers, gyroscopes, and sometimes magnetometers to measure and report a body's specific force, angular rate, and orientation
plainspeak.high-school: A sensor package with accelerometers and gyroscopes that measures motion, rotation, and orientation
plainspeak.kindergarten: A tiny motion sensor inside phones, watches, and wearables that knows which way you're moving and turning

plainspeak.doctor: Direct preference optimization using pairwise preferences to align language models with human values
plainspeak.high-school: Training AI by showing it which outputs are better and letting it learn from comparisons
plainspeak.kindergarten: Teaching AI right from wrong by example

plainspeak.doctor: Group relative policy optimization — a reinforcement learning variant that compares groups of outputs rather than individual pairs to compute advantage
plainspeak.high-school: A way to train AI by comparing groups of its outputs against each other instead of one-to-one
plainspeak.kindergarten: Teaching AI by letting it see many answers and picking the best groups

plainspeak.doctor: Reinforcement learning from human feedback — using human preferences as a reward signal to fine-tune language models
plainspeak.high-school: Training AI based on human ratings of its outputs to align with human preferences
plainspeak.kindergarten: Learning from human teachers saying 'good job' or 'try again'

plainspeak.doctor: Mixture of experts — a neural network architecture where different 'expert' subnetworks activate for different inputs, selected by a sparse gating mechanism
plainspeak.high-school: Using many specialized sub-models where only the relevant ones activate for each task, saving compute
plainspeak.kindergarten: Like having a team of specialists where only the right expert answers each question

plainspeak.doctor: Model context protocol — an open standard for connecting AI models to external tools, data sources, and services via a unified interface
plainspeak.high-school: A standard way for AI to connect to apps, databases, and web services
plainspeak.kindergarten: A universal plug for AI to use real-world tools

plainspeak.doctor: Supervised fine-tuning — training a pre-trained model on labeled examples to specialize it for a specific task or domain
plainspeak.high-school: Teaching a pre-trained AI by showing it correct examples of exactly what you want it to do
plainspeak.kindergarten: Giving the AI practice problems with the right answers to learn from

plainspeak.doctor: Low-rank adaptation — a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that trains small adaptor matrices instead of updating the full model weights
plainspeak.high-school: A memory-efficient way to customize AI models by training small add-on modules instead of retuning everything
plainspeak.kindergarten: Sticking a small custom module onto an AI instead of rebuilding it from scratch

plainspeak.doctor: Large language model -- a neural network with billions of parameters trained on vast text corpora to generate and understand human language
plainspeak.high-school: A large AI model trained on text that can read, write, reason, and follow instructions
plainspeak.kindergarten: A smart computer brain that understands words

plainspeak.doctor: Vision-and-language navigation — training agents to navigate physical environments by following natural language instructions and interpreting visual input
plainspeak.high-school: Teaching AI to move through spaces by understanding both what it sees and spoken directions
plainspeak.kindergarten: A robot that follows directions while looking where it's going

plainspeak.doctor: Reasoning and acting — a prompting framework that interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with tool-use actions for agentic tasks
plainspeak.high-school: A pattern where AI thinks step-by-step and takes actions based on each reasoning step
plainspeak.kindergarten: Think then do, think then do, one step at a time

plainspeak.doctor: Optical character recognition -- the computational process of converting images of typed, handwritten, or printed text into machine-encoded text, using pattern recognition, feature detection, and often neural networks
plainspeak.high-school: Using software to read text from images, scanned documents, or photos and converting it into editable digital text
plainspeak.kindergarten: A computer reading printed words and turning them into editable text

plainspeak.doctor: Beginner's All-purpose Symbolic Instruction Code -- a family of high-level programming languages originally designed in 1964, widely distributed through early personal computers and microcomputers, serving as the entry point for an entire generation of programmers
plainspeak.high-school: A simple, easy-to-learn programming language that came built into early personal computers, letting beginners write programs with straightforward commands
plainspeak.kindergarten: An old beginner's computer language that let people tell computers what to do by typing simple instructions

plainspeak.doctor: Multimodal large language model — a language model extended to process and reason over multiple input modalities (text, images, video, audio) simultaneously
plainspeak.high-school: A large AI model that can understand both text and images, answering questions about pictures and video
plainspeak.kindergarten: A smart computer brain that understands both words and pictures

plainspeak.doctor: Vision language model — a neural network trained jointly on image-text pairs that can perform cross-modal tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering, and grounded reasoning
plainspeak.high-school: An AI model trained on images paired with text descriptions that can understand pictures and answer questions about them
plainspeak.kindergarten: A computer brain that's trained to understand images and the words that describe them

plainspeak.doctor: Natural language generation — the subfield of AI and computational linguistics concerned with the automatic production of coherent human-like text from structured or unstructured data
plainspeak.high-school: The field of AI focused on teaching computers to write text that sounds like it was written by a person
plainspeak.kindergarten: Teaching computers to write human-like text all on their own

plainspeak.doctor: Explicitly controlling data characteristics at the start and end of training — the training trajectory is highly sensitive to data distribution at optimization boundaries, where improper data at initialization or termination can destabilize convergence or restrict downstream performance
plainspeak.high-school: Intentionally picking what kinds of examples the model sees first and last during training, because the start and end of training have disproportionate influence on what the AI learns
plainspeak.kindergarten: Carefully choosing the first and last training examples, because beginnings and endings matter the most

plainspeak.doctor: Periodically revisiting training samples across the full difficulty spectrum during one-pass training — prevents catastrophic forgetting of foundational knowledge when the model only sees each example once, by cycling through easy-to-hard patterns instead of a single monotonic progression
plainspeak.high-school: Revisiting the full range of easy-to-hard examples in cycles during training, so the AI doesn't forget basic skills as it moves to harder material
plainspeak.kindergarten: Making the AI revisit easy stuff regularly so it doesn't forget the basics while learning hard stuff

plainspeak.doctor: Maintaining smooth transitions in data attributes throughout the training sequence — abrupt fluctuations in sample difficulty can shock the optimizer, causing high gradient variance, optimization instability, and potential loss divergence
plainspeak.high-school: Making sure the difficulty of training examples changes gradually, not abruptly — sudden jumps in complexity can confuse the AI's learning process and make it unstable
plainspeak.kindergarten: Changing the difficulty of practice problems smoothly, not suddenly, so the AI doesn't get confused

plainspeak.doctor: Preserving sufficient heterogeneity among training samples within local sections of the training sequence — when mini-batch samples have nearly identical scores, gradient diversity diminishes, biasing the optimizer toward repetitive patterns and reducing the model's ability to learn generalizable features
plainspeak.high-school: Making sure each small batch of training examples contains a mix of different kinds of problems, not all the same — variety within each batch prevents the AI from overfitting to narrow patterns
plainspeak.kindergarten: Mixing up practice problems so the AI sees lots of different kinds together, not just the same thing over and over

plainspeak.doctor: Stair Ordering — a cross-guidance data ordering strategy combining Boundary Sharpening (G1), Cyclic Scheduling (G2), and Local Diversity (G4). Partitions data into stable regions (monotonic order preserved) and transition regions (interleaved via folding), with optional jittering for local diversity
plainspeak.high-school: A data ordering method that keeps a global easy-to-hard progression but inserts controlled review cycles at transition points, like stairs with periodic landings
plainspeak.kindergarten: Ordering training data like stairs — mostly going up, but with flat steps where the AI reviews what it knows

plainspeak.doctor: Saw Ordering — extends STR by integrating all four guidances (G1-G4), replacing folding in transition regions with Zig-zag ordering (ZIG) to ensure smooth continuity between region boundaries, producing a sawtooth pattern of data difficulty
plainspeak.high-school: Like STR but with smoother transitions between difficulty levels — instead of sudden jumps, it creates a continuous sawtooth pattern that avoids shocking the optimizer
plainspeak.kindergarten: Like stair ordering but extra smooth — the AI slides up and down gently instead of jumping between hard and easy
